tools.name: OCR
tools.description: This tool can recognize all text on the input image.

outputs.description: OCR results, include bbox in x1, y1, x2, y2 format and the recognized text.

tools.name: ImageDescription
tools.description: A useful tool that returns a brief description of the input image.

tools.name: RegionAttributeDescription
tools.description: Describe the attribute of a region of the input image.

inputs.name: bbox
inputs.description: The bbox coordinate in the format of `(x1, y1, x2, y2)`

tools.name: GoogleSearch
tools.description: Searches the web for brief reference information to interpret marker meaning in histology.

inputs.name: k
inputs.description: Number of results or snippets to return

dialogs.content: Using the provided histology figure, what are the three types of lesions observed in the mammary gland, and how are they characterized?

dialogs.thought: First read the figure labels to identify how many lesion classes are shown and how the panels are organized.
Now understand the overall layout so I can relate each lesion type to wholemount appearance, H&E histology, and ZO-1/laminin staining.

content.content: A multi-panel scientific figure (panel A) arranged in three rows labeled Type I, Type II, and Type III, and three columns labeled Wholemount, H&E, and ZO-1/Laminin. Each row shows a different mammary gland lesion type with corresponding wholemount morphology, H&E histology, and immunofluorescence staining.

arguments.query: ZO-1 laminin mammary gland histology epithelial polarity basement membrane staining lesions

dialogs.thought: Before interpreting the immunofluorescence panels, clarify what ZO-1 and laminin mark so the staining can be linked to lesion architecture and invasiveness.

content.content: ZO-1 is a tight-junction marker associated with apical epithelial cell borders and polarity, while laminin highlights the basement membrane surrounding ducts/acini; together they help distinguish organized polarized epithelial structures from disorganized invasive lesions.

arguments.attribute: ZO-1/laminin staining pattern and epithelial organization in Type I row

dialogs.thought: Inspect Type I to see whether it preserves organized epithelial polarity and a clear basement membrane, which would support a less advanced lesion.

content.content: In the Type I ZO-1/Laminin panel, there are round/oval duct-like structures with a clear central lumen; a continuous green rim outlines the basement membrane (laminin) and red signal highlights apical junctional staining consistent with an organized polarized epithelial lining.

arguments.attribute: ZO-1/laminin staining pattern and lumen appearance in Type II row

dialogs.thought: Inspect Type II to determine whether the epithelium becomes multilayered with smaller or collapsed lumens while still retaining some ductal association.

content.content: In the Type II ZO-1/Laminin panel, the field is more cellular with less obvious open luminal spaces; staining appears more fragmented/patchy around small ductal profiles, with many nuclei and relatively collapsed or small lumens compared with Type I.

arguments.attribute: ZO-1/laminin expression and invasiveness impression in Type III row

dialogs.thought: Inspect Type III for loss of polarity and basement membrane staining, which would indicate an invasive lesion.

content.content: In the Type III ZO-1/Laminin panel, the image is dominated by dense blue nuclei with minimal to absent red/green junctional/basement-membrane outlining; there is no clear organized ductal lumen, suggesting loss of epithelial polarity/architecture.

arguments.attribute: compare ZO-1 and laminin staining across the mammary gland lesion types and identify which lesions show organized versus disrupted epithelial and basement-membrane patterns

dialogs.thought: Synthesize the row-wise differences to characterize each lesion type from organized polarized structures to invasive disorganized lesions.

content.content: Across the three lesion types, Type I shows organized polarized epithelial structures with clear lumens and intact basement membrane, Type II shows more cellular multilayered duct-associated lesions with reduced or collapsed lumens and less uniform staining, and Type III shows disorganized invasive cellular masses with loss of clear ZO-1 and laminin patterning.

dialogs.content: The three histologically distinct lesions observed in the mammary gland are Type I, Type II, and Type III lesions. Type I lesions are characterized by the punctate appearance of lateral buds lining the ductal epithelium and contain a single layer of polarized mammary epithelial cells with large distinct lumens. Type II lesions have a uniform multicellular epithelium with small collapsed lumens and are closely associated with primary ducts. Type III lesions are multicellular, invasive, well vascularized, and have lost ZO-1 and laminin expression. They are often associated with surrounding leukocyte infiltration and reactive stroma.

whitelist: The three histologically distinct lesions observed in the mammary gland are Type I, Type II, and Type III lesions. Type I lesions are characterized by the punctate appearance of lateral buds lining the ductal epithelium and contain a single layer of polarized mammary epithelial cells with large distinct lumens. Type II lesions have a uniform multicellular epithelium with small collapsed lumens and are closely associated with primary ducts. Type III lesions are multicellular, invasive, well vascularized, and have lost ZO-1 and laminin expression. They are often associated with surrounding leukocyte infiltration and reactive stroma.
